text: In the days of Amraphel, Arioch, Chedorlaomer, and Tidal, these kings made war.
They fought against the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela (Zoar).
These five kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim, by the Salt Sea.
They had served Chedorlaomer twelve years, but rebelled in the thirteenth.
In the fourteenth year, Chedorlaomer and his allied kings came and defeated several peoples along the way.
They also defeated the Horites in their hill country, as far as El-paran near the wilderness.
They turned back and struck the Amalekites and the Amorites living in Hazazon-tamar.
The kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela went out to fight them in the Valley of Siddim.
Four kings fought against five in that valley.
The valley was full of tar pits; the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled and fell into them, and the rest fled to the hills.
The victors seized all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their food, and went their way.
They also took Lot, Abram's nephew, who lived in Sodom, along with his possessions.
A survivor came and told Abram the Hebrew, who was camped by the oaks of Mamre; Mamre, Eshcol, and Aner were Abram's allies.
When Abram heard his nephew had been captured, he armed 318 trained men born in his household and pursued them as far as Dan.
He divided his forces by night against them and struck them down, chasing them to Hobah, north of Damascus.
He brought back all the goods, and also his nephew Lot with his possessions, along with the women and other captives.
After his return, the king of Sodom went out to meet Abram in the Valley of Shaveh, the King's Valley.
Melchizedek, king of Salem, brought out bread and wine; he was a priest of God Most High.
He blessed Abram, saying, blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth.
He blessed God Most High, who delivered Abram's enemies into his hand; and Abram gave him a tenth of everything.
The king of Sodom told Abram to keep the goods but give him back the people.
Abram told the king of Sodom he had sworn an oath to the LORD, Possessor of heaven and earth.
He would take nothing, not even a thread or sandal strap, so no one could say they had made Abram rich.
He would take nothing except what his young men had eaten and the share of his allies Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre.
